chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum in libUbikeyJni.so; no allocator/sanitizer frames shown, so this is the first app-level function.
Name strongly suggests it walks an ASN.1 structure to compute child element count; typical failure modes include out-of-bounds parsing when length fields or nesting are malformed.

Call stack shows a clear parsing pipeline: CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. These are all app/library routines, not harness utilities.

Fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input presumably supplies arbitrary certificate/ASN.1 data down to CERT_Load, simulating untrusted input (e.g., server certificate or token). Thus, ASN.1 contents are attacker-controlled in realistic use as well.

Even without decompilation, an app-native crash during ASN.1 child-count computation likely indicates missing bounds checks on lengths/offsets, leading to memory corruption (read/write OOB) or invalid dereference.

Because this is a banking/smartbank app and the functions resemble custom or bundled crypto/parsing code, malformed certificates or tokens from a network peer or file could reach this path in production.

No evidence that the crash is due solely to an artificial harness condition (e.g., null env, invalid JNI object); stack is a normal Java->JNI->native parse flow, with the harness only acting as Java caller.

Given typical ASN.1 parser issues and the fuzzing context, this termination is best classified as a likely genuine memory-safety vulnerability, though exact primitive (OOB read vs write) is unknown from the trace text alone.

Severity is at least high because ASN.1 parsing bugs frequently enable controlled heap corruption or denial-of-service, and are often exploitable; however, without exact crash type or code snippet, I cannot assert RCE (critical) with >0.9 confidence.
Assigning CWE-125/787 class; but to stay conservative, I’ll pick generic out-of-bounds read/write CWE-125 or improper input validation CWE-20 coupled with unsafe memory operations.
reasons: Crash occurs in custom ASN.1 parsing routine Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, not in harness glue or allocator
Call chain shows attacker-controlled ASN.1/certificate data flowing from fuzz harness into CERT_Load and ASN.1 converters without evidence of strict validation
ASN.1 child-number computation is a classic source of out-of-bounds indexing when length and nesting fields are malformed
Fuzzing context plus normal-looking JNI stack suggests a real parsing bug rather than an artificial harness-only fault
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum
recommendations: Review and harden ASN.1 parsing in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related Convert_ASN1_* functions with strict length and bounds checks before accessing child elements
Enable and run with ASan/ubsan on libUbikeyJni.so while re-running the fuzzer to pinpoint exact out-of-bounds access and confirm whether it’s read or write
Consider replacing custom ASN.1/X.509 parsing with a well-reviewed implementation (e.g., BoringSSL/OpenSSL) or at least add rigorous fuzz regression tests around CERT_Load
assumptions: Fuzz harness passes arbitrary ASN.1 or certificate bytes that mimic realistic untrusted inputs such as network-provided certs or tokens
No prior strict ASN.1 validation occurs in Java before calling jGetOrgName
Process termination is due to a memory-safety fault (e.g., invalid access) rather than an intentional abort on parse error
limitations: No direct decompiled code or crash signal (SIGSEGV vs abort) available to confirm precise bug type
JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName implementation and CERT_Load input contract not inspected
Lack of concrete input sample limits assessment of exploit primitives (heap layout, write vs read)

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a maliciously crafted ASN.1/X.509 certificate or token that is parsed via UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse an attacker-supplied certificate or ASN.1 blob (e.g., via network connection, QR file, or imported credential)
Vulnerable native library libUbikeyJni.so is present and used in the code path
No upstream validation rejects malformed ASN.1 before reaching Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum
exploit_pipeline: Prepare an ASN.1/X.509 object with malformed length and child element metadata to drive Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum into out-of-bounds access
Deliver this object through the app feature that ultimately invokes UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName (e.g., certificate import or authentication handshake)
Trigger the parsing so that CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is executed
Observe crash or memory corruption and iteratively tune the payload to influence heap layout and control potential overwrite targets
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.kbankwith.smartbank/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der  # assumes an activity loads certs from this extra
exploit.notes: Exact UI/IPC entrypoint into UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName is unknown; PoC assumes a feature that imports or processes certificates, and that this path eventually calls the JNI method with untrusted ASN.1 data.

chain_of_thought: Top app frame is Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName in libUbikeyJni.so; crash is scudo invalid chunk state on deallocate, indicating heap corruption or double free before abort().

No inner native frames are shown, so corruption happened earlier in jGetOrgName or a callee; but stack does not provide more details and Ghidra access failed, so we cannot inspect implementation or data flow or see how arguments come from JNI/Java.

However, this is application allocator (scudo) detecting misuse triggered directly from an app JNI function, not from the fuzz harness, and the function name suggests it fetches an organization name, likely processing input from Java (potentially user or server data).

Scudo::reportInvalidChunkState is almost always due to use-after-free, double free, or heap metadata overwrite, which are canonical memory safety vulnerabilities (CWE-415/416/787) and not benign harness issues by themselves.

The fuzz harness simply calls jGetOrgName; there is no evidence that it passes impossible arguments like invalid JNIEnv, so the most reasonable interpretation is that app code mishandles its own heap and the crash would also occur in production under certain inputs.

Because we lack concrete source/assembly, we cannot assert exploitability as fully practical (no clear attacker control over sizes/pointers), but allocator-detected heap misuse in app JNI is strongly suggestive of a real vulnerability, with at least denial-of-service and possibly code execution.

Therefore classify as vulnerable with medium-high confidence, high severity due to memory corruption potential, but keep exploitability marked as theoretical and note assumptions (e.g., reachable from untrusted data paths).
reasons: Crash is scudo allocator abort due to invalid chunk state during free/deallocate, indicating heap corruption or double free in native code.
The failure occurs in app JNI function Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName within libUbikeyJni.so, not just in the fuzz harness.
Allocator-detected heap misuse (invalid chunk) is a strong indicator of memory safety issues that can lead to denial-of-service or potential code execution.
No evidence that the crash is purely due to unrealistic harness parameters such as invalid JNIEnv; the path is consistent with production JNI usage.
call_sequence: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName -> scudo::Allocator<scudo::AndroidConfig, &scudo_malloc_postinit>::deallocate -> scudo::reportInvalidChunkState -> scudo::ScopedErrorReport::~ScopedErrorReport -> scudo::die -> abort
recommendations: Statically and dynamically analyze Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName and its callees for incorrect free/delete, double free, or heap buffer overruns before deallocation.
Run the app under ASan/Scudo with full error reports enabled to capture exact allocation and free sites for the invalid chunk.
Add rigorous ownership tracking and avoid manual free of memory managed elsewhere (e.g., JVM, shared buffers); ensure each allocation is freed exactly once and only after last use.
assumptions: JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName is reachable via normal app flows, not only via a special fuzz-only harness.
Fuzz harness passes valid JNIEnv and jobject arguments, so allocator crash reflects real misuse inside libUbikeyJni.so code.
Scudo invalid chunk state reflects app heap misuse (double free/UAF/OOB) rather than a bug in the allocator itself.
limitations: Could not inspect native implementation of Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName due to tooling limitations, so exact root cause and attacker-controlled parameters are unknown.
Java-side call graph and input sources to jGetOrgName were not available, so reachability from untrusted data is inferred, not proven.
No precise scudo error log (e.g., allocation stack, corruption report) to distinguish between double free and buffer overflow.

prerequisites: Ability to invoke the code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName in the target app.
Control over some input (e.g., account/organization identifiers or server responses) that influences how jGetOrgName allocates and frees memory.
exploit_pipeline: Reverse-engineer libUbikeyJni.so to identify how jGetOrgName processes inputs and manages heap allocations.
Identify an input-dependent path where a buffer is freed twice or heap metadata is corrupted before free, reproducing the scudo invalid chunk crash.
Craft inputs that reliably hit this path and, if possible, adjust heap layout to control adjacent allocations.
Attempt to move from simple crash to controlled overwrite of heap metadata or adjacent objects, enabling code execution or information disclosure.
exploit.notes: PoC details depend on the actual Java entry point and input channel that reach UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName; placeholders are used for package, activity, and test runner names.

chain_of_thought:
Native stack shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → CERT_Load → JNI jGetOrgName. The first app-level native frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so. Tools are unavailable (404), so I cannot decompile, but I must still reason from naming and typical patterns: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly suggests ASN.1 DER decoding, usually on certificate blobs; CERT_Load aligns with loading/parsing a certificate. memcpy crash indicates buffer overflow or out-of-bounds read/write inside ASN.1 parsing or certificate loading. This is classic for malformed length fields in ASN.1 structures.
Given the fuzz harness, fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary data into the same JNI path Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName that production code will call with certificate data obtained from smartcard/USB token or network. In a real app, these certificate blobs may originate from untrusted sources (e.g., external token, server, or file). ASN.1 decoders must treat the encoded lengths as untrusted; if Decode_DER_ASN1_ST misvalidates length fields and passes incorrect size to memcpy, that is a memory safety vulnerability.
Even though I cannot see code, the crash primitive (memcpy) + function naming (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST) + fuzz context (binary input) strongly suggest an unchecked memcpy based on parsed length. There is no evidence this is a harness-only misuse like passing NULL; the harness simply routes fuzz input into the same CERT_Load / Decode_DER_ASN1_ST as the app. Therefore, I treat this as a likely out-of-bounds write/read due to insufficient bounds checking of DER length fields. This fits CWE-787 / CWE-119, potentially exploitable for code execution depending on heap layout.
Severity: high, since memcpy-based overflow in certificate parsing reachable by attacker-controlled cert data is classically exploitable. Confidence: not maximum because I lack direct code, but naming and pattern are strong; I choose around 0.7. JNI bridge method is given; Java callgraph not provided, but we know entry is fuzz_one_input → JNI. Call sequence is reconstructed from stack trace. Affected library: libUbikeyJni.so. Exploitability: at least theoretical; attacker can supply crafted DER certificate to the component using UbikeyJni to read an organization name from a smartcard/cert, likely via some UI or network channel. PoC: generic Android adb shell am start or file push, but concrete activity not known, so I have to use placeholders and document assumptions.
reasons: Crash occurs in libc __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load, indicating unsafe memory copy during certificate/ASN.1 decoding.
Function names (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load) and fuzzing context strongly indicate parsing of attacker-controllable DER/ASN.1 data with inadequate bounds checking.
JNI path Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrgName suggests native parsing is triggered from Java using external certificate/token data, making the bug reachable in realistic scenarios.
recommendations: Audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load for all memcpy/memmove calls and ensure lengths are validated against actual buffer sizes and remaining input.
Add strict DER/ASN.1 bounds checking, including nested length fields, and return errors instead of copying when fields exceed available data.
Instrument with ASan/UBSan in a debug build and re-fuzz CERT_Load / Decode_DER_ASN1_ST with coverage-guided fuzzing to confirm fixes and catch similar issues.
assumptions: Certificate or ASN.1 data processed by CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is at least partially attacker-controlled in production (e.g., from external token, network, or file).
The memcpy crash is due to out-of-bounds access rather than a deliberate abort inside libc.
The fuzz harness invokes the same JNI / native code paths as the real app without adding unrealistic arguments (e.g., always-null pointers).
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so due to tool access errors, so argument-level data flow to memcpy is inferred, not proven.
No Java callgraph or manifest context to pinpoint exact external entrypoints or how untrusted data reaches jGetOrgName.
Allocator or sanitizer diagnostics (exact faulting address and size) are unavailable, limiting precise classification (read vs write, heap vs stack).

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a maliciously crafted DER/ASN.1 certificate blob to code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName
prerequisites: Attacker can influence or provide certificate/ASN.1 data consumed by CERT_Load (e.g., via external token, imported certificate file, or network response).
Target device runs a build using the vulnerable libUbikeyJni.so without hardening that prevents exploitation (e.g., full ASLR/CFI may raise difficulty but not eliminate risk).
exploit_pipeline: Identify app feature that loads or displays certificate organization name using UbikeyJni.jGetOrgName (e.g., smartcard login or certificate management screen).
Craft a DER/ASN.1 certificate with malformed or oversized length fields that cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an incorrect copy length for memcpy.
Deliver the crafted certificate to the app via the supported channel (e.g., import certificate file, provision smartcard, or MITM network response if applicable).
Trigger the feature that calls jGetOrgName so CERT_Load parses the malicious certificate and reaches the vulnerable memcpy, causing memory corruption and potential code execution.
exploit.notes: Exact file paths, package, and activity names are unknown; replace <PACKAGE>/<ACTIVITY> and intent extras with the actual entrypoint used by the app to import or process certificates.
